planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in tropical or subtropical climates (zone 9b–12) in full sun in moist, well-drained, fertile soil; Egg Fruit (Canistel, Tiesa) is a medium-sized evergreen sapotaceous tree native to Central America and Mexico bearing oval to pear-shaped fruits with smooth, vivid orange-yellow skin and dense, dry, sweet, orange-yellow flesh of a texture and richness so similar to a hard-boiled egg yolk that it is called Egg Fruit in English and compared to sweet potato in flavor.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Pouteria campechiana benefits from regular water during fruiting but tolerates brief drought once established — maintain good drainage and avoid standing water around the root zone. Pouteria campechiana (Egg Fruit, Canistel, or Tiesa) is an evergreen or briefly deciduous tree native to the tropical forests of Central America — Guatemala, Belize, Mexico's Yucatan Peninsula — and introduced throughout the Caribbean, South Florida, the Philippines, Southeast Asia, and tropical West Africa. The fruit is one of the most distinctive of the sapote family: ovoid to pear-shaped, 2–4 inches long, with smooth, waxy, vivid orange to golden-yellow skin, and dense, dry, smooth, richly sweet orange-yellow flesh with a starchy texture remarkably similar to a hard-boiled egg yolk — sometimes described as sweet potato crossed with pumpkin pie. The flesh is eaten fresh, made into egg fruit nog, smoothies, ice cream, jam, and pies; it is particularly popular in Cuba, the Philippines, and South Florida ethnic markets. Trees require grafted or air-layered specimens to fruit true-to-type and within 3–5 years; seedling trees may take 6–8 years to fruit. A productive and adaptable tropical fruit tree for South Florida, the Gulf Coast, and similar warm subtropical climates.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Tropical Research and Education Center — Canistel
Morton, J. — Fruits of Warm Climates (1987)

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in full sun in moist, well-drained, fertile to average soil in zone 9–11; Wooly-leaf White Sapote is a close relative of the common White Sapote (Casimiroa edulis) native to highland Mexico and Guatemala, somewhat hardier to cold, with distinctively soft-woolly leaf undersides and the same smooth, green-skinned fruits with creamy, sweet, custardy white flesh — considered by some sapote growers to have superior flavor to the more common C. edulis.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, fertile to average, neutral to slightly acidic soil; Casimiroa tetrameria tolerates brief light frost once established — provide winter irrigation in dry climates and mulch the root zone against cold. Casimiroa tetrameria (Wooly-leaf White Sapote or Matasano) is an evergreen to briefly deciduous tree native to the highlands of central Mexico and Guatemala, closely related to the more widely cultivated White Sapote (Casimiroa edulis) and distinguished by its distinctively velvety-woolly leaf undersides (the species name 'tetrameria' references the 4-merous flowers, while the woolly leaves differentiate it visually from C. edulis). The smooth, round to oval, 2–3 inch, green-skinned fruits contain creamy-white to cream-yellow, smooth, dense, rich, sweet flesh with a buttery-custard flavor and soft, satiny texture — eaten fresh, chilled, or pureed into smoothies and ice cream. Casimiroa tetrameria is considered by some tropical fruit enthusiasts to produce sweeter, richer fruit than the common white sapote, and the tree shows somewhat improved cold tolerance, performing well in zone 9 with good drainage. Graft onto Casimiroa edulis rootstock for earlier fruiting and better uniformity. The seeds contain mildly sedative alkaloids (casimiroin) and should not be eaten; wash hands after seed handling.
sources: California Rare Fruit Growers — Casimiroa tetrameria
Morton, J. — Fruits of Warm Climates (1987)

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in tropical or subtropical climates (zone 10–12) in full sun in well-drained, fertile, slightly alkaline to neutral soil; Spanish Lime (Genip, Mamoncillo, Quenepa) is a large, handsome, long-lived tropical evergreen tree native to the Caribbean and northern South America producing clusters of small, round, lime-green, thin-shelled fruits with a sweet-sour, salmon-to-orange, firm, jelly-like pulp clinging tightly to the seed — eaten by sucking or scooping the translucent pulp, a beloved street food of the Caribbean.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, fertile, alkaline-tolerant soil; Melicoccus bijugatus is drought tolerant and adapted to calcareous, rocky, and coastal soils typical of the Caribbean — water regularly only in establishment and during flowering and fruit set. Melicoccus bijugatus (Spanish Lime, Genip, Mamoncillo, Quenepa, or Limoncillo) is a large, stately, long-lived evergreen tree native to Colombia, Venezuela, and the Lesser Antilles, now widely naturalized and cultivated throughout the Caribbean, Central America, and parts of Florida and Hawaii. The common name 'Spanish Lime' describes the fruit's appearance — small (1–1.5 inch), perfectly round, lime-green, thin-shelled, resembling a small lime — but the flavor is a sweet-sour, tropical, guava-lychee combination with firm, translucent salmon-to-orange jelly-like pulp clinging tightly to the large seed. Fruits are sold in street markets in enormous clusters of hundreds — eaten by cracking the thin shell between the teeth and sucking or scraping the juicy pulp from the seed. Spanish lime is one of the most beloved street foods and market fruits of the Caribbean, Puerto Rico, Cuba, and Colombia. The ripe fruits are also made into juice, jams, wine, and fermented beverages. Trees are dioecious (separate male and female plants), so plant at least one male per several females for fruiting. Very long-lived — specimens 100+ years old still fruit productively. Well adapted to South Florida's alkaline limestone soils where many tropical fruits perform poorly.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Mamoncillo (Spanish Lime)
Morton, J. — Fruits of Warm Climates (1987)

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in tropical climates (zone 11–12) in partial shade to full sun in moist, fertile, humus-rich, well-drained, acidic soil; African Bush Mango (Ozigo) is a large rainforest tree of Central and West Africa in the same genus as Safou (D. edulis) — bearing elongated, oblong, oil-rich, dark-blue to black fruits boiled and eaten as a sauce or condiment in Central African Gabonese and Cameroonian forest communities.

directions: Plant in moist to wet, fertile, humus-rich, well-drained, acidic soil in partial shade to full sun; Dacryodes buettneri requires consistently high rainfall and humidity typical of tropical rainforest conditions — water abundantly during dry spells, mulch heavily, and maintain high organic matter content. Dacryodes buettneri (African Bush Mango, Ozigo, or Arbre à Beurre in French) is a large, emergent to canopy rainforest tree native to Gabon, Cameroon, Equatorial Guinea, and the Congo Basin forests, a member of the same Burseraceae genus as the widely grown African olive (Dacryodes edulis / Safou). Like safou, D. buettneri produces oblong, oil-rich fruits with thin, waxy skin ripening from green to dark-blue or black-purple, cooked (boiled or roasted) rather than eaten raw — the soft, creamy, rich, butter-like pulp develops a flavor somewhat reminiscent of avocado or a rich olive when heated. The fruits are an important food and oil source for forest-dwelling communities in Gabon and Cameroon, and the extracted fat is used for cooking and traditional personal care. Dacryodes buettneri is a tall-growing, high-humidity, tropical rainforest species best suited to humid equatorial climates in zones 11–12; it does not tolerate drought, salt, or cold and needs rich, moist, forest-type soils to perform well.
sources: CIFOR Forests and Livelihoods — Dacryodes buettneri
Kew Seed Information Database — Dacryodes buettneri

planting.note: Divide rhizomes or plant container stock in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, acidic soil in deep to moderate shade in tropical or subtropical climates (zone 10–12); Black Bat Flower is a shade-loving tropical rhizomatous perennial native to the humid forests of Thailand, Myanmar, Yunnan, Malaysia, and Borneo, producing extraordinary, bat-shaped, deep maroon-black to purple-black flowers with long, whisker-like purple-black bracts up to 12 inches long — one of the most dramatically bizarre flowers in the plant kingdom.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, acidic to slightly acidic soil in deep shade to bright indirect light; Tacca chantrieri demands consistent high humidity, warmth, and shade — it shuts down in direct sun or drought conditions and is best grown under a dense canopy or in a sheltered, humid, north- or east-facing position. Tacca chantrieri (Black Bat Flower, Devil Flower, or Cat's Whiskers) is a dramatic, clump-forming, shade-loving rhizomatous perennial native to the humid tropical forests of mainland Southeast Asia — Thailand, Myanmar, Laos, Cambodia, Yunnan Province (China), and extending into Borneo and the Malay Peninsula — where it grows in deep forest floor understory in high humidity, filtered light, and rich, moist, leaf-litter soil. The flowers are among the most extraordinary in nature: large, bat-shaped umbels of deepest maroon-black to purple-black, 4–8 inch flowers subtended by similarly dark, wing-like bracts, and accompanied by impossibly long (up to 12–28 inches), threadlike, drooping, black-purple 'whiskers' (bracteoles) that give the plant its common names. The flowers rise singly on long peduncles above the large, broad, glossy, tropical-looking leaves. Tacca chantrieri is challenging to grow outside of tropical climates — it requires high humidity (above 60%), warmth (minimum 60°F), consistent moisture without waterlogging, and deep shade or bright indirect light only. Excellent for tropical shade gardens, humid greenhouse cultivation, and indoor specimen planting in warm, bright interiors. Repot annually and divide clumps when crowded.
sources: Royal Botanic Gardens Kew — Tacca chantrieri
Florida Master Gardener — Growing Tacca in Florida

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost or plant container stock in full sun in moist to wet, fertile soil in zone 6–10; Scarlet Rosemallow is a tall, spectacular native perennial of the coastal marshes, swamps, and wet bottomlands of coastal Georgia, Florida, Alabama, and Louisiana, producing enormous, pure scarlet-red, dinner-plate-sized flowers all summer on tall (5–8 foot) stems — one of the showiest native plants of the southeastern coastal plain.

directions: Plant in full sun in moist to wet, fertile, organically rich soil; Hibiscus coccineus thrives in consistently moist to wet conditions and is ideal for pond edges, rain gardens, and moist low spots — keep consistently moist in summer, water heavily during hot dry periods, and mulch thickly to retain soil moisture. Hibiscus coccineus (Scarlet Rosemallow, Texas Star, or Texas Star Hibiscus) is a tall, robust, herbaceous perennial native to the coastal marshes, tidal swamps, freshwater wetlands, and stream banks of coastal Georgia, Florida (especially the panhandle and northern peninsula), Alabama, and Louisiana — one of the most visually spectacular native plants of the southeastern coastal plain. The enormous, pure scarlet-crimson, 5-petaled flowers — up to 6–8 inches across, with no color variation — are borne prolifically from late June through September on 5–8 foot upright stems above deeply cut, maple-leaf-like, dark-green foliage that is itself ornamentally attractive. The combination of bold tropical-looking foliage and brilliant scarlet flowers over a long summer season makes it one of the most outstanding native perennials for large garden settings, waterside plantings, and bird gardens — hummingbirds and bumblebees work the flowers continuously. Plants die completely to the ground in winter (even in zones 9–10) and re-emerge in late spring; mark locations to avoid disturbing dormant crowns. Excellent for Gulf Coast rain gardens, pond margins, and naturalistic native plantings.
sources: Florida Native Plant Society — Hibiscus coccineus
Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center — Hibiscus coccineus
